sentences.text: In my previous part of the explanation, I came to the point of my father's occupation.
It is relevant, insofar as to understand the background of the implied and practiced anti-Semitism, which obviously existed.

Nevertheless, my late uncle, my father's older brother, the lawyer, was such a prominent person that at one stage it was suggested that he should become lord mayor, which of course in the 1920s was out of the question.
A, he wouldn't have accepted it, and b, he would have never been suitable for it as a Jew.

Nevertheless, because of his standing in the community-- in the general community, I'm not talking about the Jewish community-- and his record in the courts and so on, being a very successful lawyer, he had extremely good connections, because in fact he was sort of supportive of a more democratic type of political party.
The reason I mention that is-- besides the personal anecdote-- is that he in turn had the influence in the town hall.
And my father's livelihood or occupation after the first war was greatly enhanced by this fact, because as I told you, he went into the real estate business.

And whilst he was quite financial, and also having married the daughter of a very wealthy person from [INAUDIBLE], my grandfather also, or grandparents, he went into partnership with two ladies who I remember very clearly, non-Jewish ladies.
And they were known to be the wealthiest people in town, and literally so.
I personally knew them.
And I remember they used to come along sort of every three or six months to consult with my father.
Maybe he was reporting on their joint ventures.
And basically, what it consisted was that they bought up large tracks of agricultural land immediately in the vicinity of the town center or just the part which was going to develop.
They-- subsequently my father subdivided it, putting roads, built houses on it, some of it very industrial premises, which were let out like a timber yard and so on.
And so much so that in the times of the 1940s, when Jews were basically being honed-- hounded in business and people didn't want to deal with them, we lived very comfortably.

Besides certain property investments, which brought a fixed income, also my father used to say that if we sell one plot of land, which we have many of them, we can live more comfortable than the amount of money we can spend.
So basically I had a-- maybe if you like to call it pampered, but a very comfortable, financially comfortable, background.
And the family was one of the foremost families in town, due to the maybe education, maybe financial influence.

So I probably had more opportunity to meet people who were non-Jewish people who my father had dealings with, than let's say a simple shopkeeper who was serving textiles or fruits or whatever in the main thoroughfare.
The community, as such, was very interesting.
It was quite separated, the Neolog and Orthodox communities, so much so that they had each their own butcher shop.
They were next door to each other.
They had all their own separate communal organizations.
They had separate cemeteries, even though they were next door to each other, which were separated by a wall.
And some cynics said that-- it was very heavily bombed, that town, in 1944, after our deportation-- that one wall, which was separated, the bomb hit it.
That was some sort of providence that communities shouldn't split.
But the bottom line, what I'm trying to say, is that my father was the head of the chevra kadisha, which is the burial society.

And it's interesting to note, for those who didn't live in that part of the world, or even some who did but who were maybe too young to know, or the too old ones are not around, it was a by government authorized autonomous communities, separately the Orthodox and the Neolog communities.
And the existence was basically through a charter, a government charter.
In Hungary, in my time certainly, every individual whether Jew or non-Jew, had to belong to a religious community.
If they happened to be-- which was basically ostracized, say they are non-believers, they also had what they called Unitarian community.
The majority of Hungary was Catholic.
That was the mainstream.
There were some Protestants in great minority and Jews also in relative minority.
I mean, as I think I mentioned, in our town of 60,000 people, there were 1,800 Jews.
We certainly lived a very modern, Westernized life.
Shtetls I never seen in my life until I got to Israel and I've seen it in the [INAUDIBLE] on videos.
And generally, I would say that we-- I can't say that I had a cultural shock after the war having left from there, because of the general lifestyle which we had.
Well, you mean personally?
Behaviorwise, I was known as a little bit of a terror.
And I was always inclined to do work.
I was told that-- I had an older brother, who was six years older by name of Erwin, who was a very gentle boy, who was tremendously good in academic field.
I remember I was told when he did his matric in mathematics, he came out as the first and he got top marks in everything.
You know, that goes within a limited time, but if you finished, you leave the room.
And there is, for instance, an interesting anecdote.
We-- we went to the Szechenyi Istvan gymnasium, which was a very highly held grammar school-- gymnasium they called it, a state gymnasium.

And I remember the story being told by my father that it was customary that when child goes to school, you go and inquire from the particular teachers in the grammar school how he is proceeding-- progressing.
And the open days, as it's known in English schools, didn't exist, so the parent had to show interest and make visits there, time to time, by appointment and make inquiries.
So there was a-- a very, very capable mathematics teacher in the Szechenyi Istvan gymnasium, who in fact taught me also.

And my father, he was born in 1924, so he would have started in 1934 school there, so it was in the period between 1934 and '38 that this happened, because afterwards he went to a specialized academy where he did his matric.
That my father one day went in to inquire, how is my son Erwin Weiler doing?
And the teacher, who was quite a nice person, there was nothing wrong with him, answered him, Mr.
Weiler, I only know two sorts of Jews, either very clever or very stupid.
You've got nothing to worry about.
What I am trying to tell a little anecdote, that how open and unashamed the anti-Semitism was.
You know, this was a sort of a friendly gesture.
It was nothing bad.
It was nothing insulting.
This was the method which was used.
I already went to school from 1936 onwards.
And in the same street from the main square, which was called Szechenyi there, was the Jewish school.
Opposite was this gymnasium, which I went to later.
And behind it was a Catholic primary school and also up to the age of 14 school.

And I remember very clearly that as we used to come out from school in my time, which was between 1936 and 1940, when I went to primary school, those children from Catholic school left school at the same time, 1 o'clock.
And there was constantly-- we were sort of fist-fight-wise, you know, like children do, attacked by sometime individual, sometime a group.
And we used to carry leather school cases on our back.

And I remember that we got into the practice of putting that on the other way, in others words, like a Roman shield in front of you and come out of school with our hands in gloves and ready for a fight.
It didn't happen very often.
It wasn't too serious.

It could have happened that if non-Jews would have stopped it, because they would not have been necessarily aware who was a Jew or wasn't, because it wasn't customary, in our part of the world, that people went with long beards and payos and children.
They were literally unrecognizable in the main, except for a few extremely religious families.
So even they could have stopped it, because it was just some disturbance on the street.
I mean, there were no knives or no serious injuries.
But it was the general norm of life.
And one accepted it.
I mean, we lived approximately 15 to 20 minutes from the synagogue.
And as a child, we always had bicycles.
And we used to go with a bike to the synagogue during the week.

And we-- even in later years, I must say that whilst there were some attacks on Jews, individual sort of events, on the whole in our town, it wasn't too bad, because the majority-- it was a German minority there, Volksdeutsche which they called them, and the Hungarians.
So they were [INAUDIBLE] at each other all the time.
The small number of Jews, which was literally 3%, 4% of the population, got by because, you know, they were busy cursing each other.
But when it came to Jews, they saw eye-to-eye on it.
But there was less attention given to the persecution of the Jews in our town than in some other parts of towns, where in fact, even after the war, pogroms have happened.
So there was a relative security or implied security, which under the-- illusion under which we lived.
We-- interesting part of it is that the-- the worst antagonism and origin of the constantly keeping it on a daily basis, the anti-Semitism and its results, came from the Catholic priests.
There's no question about it.

I remember very clearly that it is customary, as I'm quite informed of the Catholic practices, because we lived in a Catholic religious country, that during Easter period, there are certain processions taking place.
Now I must emphasize that in that time, it wasn't a question of personal safety to walk on the streets.
It was all right.
However, when those processions were taking place-- and that was on weekdays, and I was going, let's say, at 8 o'clock in the morning to school, or coming home from school.

And that procession was-- it was advisable for one's own safety to wait 5 or 10 minutes in a doorway of a house, or go inside the doorway of the house, and wait 'til the procession went, because the whole atmosphere was created-- you Jews killed our God and this is the time of the year and you are responsible for it.
Now needless to say, not every member of a community-- of a city are intelligent, normal-thinking people and bigotry takes roots very quickly.
And of course, the more uneducated ruffians took heed of the priest's words.
And if I may transgress a long period of time, because this point I very much would like to make.
In 1946, after the war, after all what had happened, I lived in the town of my grandparents with my uncles in Szombathely, which was well known to me, because I was the only survivor in my family.

I clearly remember that the Hungarian Primate, whose name is Mindszenty, was internationally known because he took refuge in the 1956 revolution in the American embassy for 15 years, made a speech in Hungarian, in the main square-- Fo ter it was called, I can give you practically the date in 1946-- in which he went on to say, I cannot understand all the commotion which is being created that so many Jews got killed.
After all, there was a war.
Many Hungarians got killed in many other nations.
Maybe a somewhat larger percentage got killed, but I'm not aware of anything serious.
Now, I think this is the cause of the Holocaust.

These are the people who are not idiots, who are clever, educated, intelligent people, knowingly over the periods of time after the war-- let it be what was before the war-- still had the audacity to make such a statement.
And that is, I think, what people in future generations should remember.

Now coming back to the period of relative tranquility in the '30s, I remember that the first serious anti-Jewish legislations were brought in 1935, '36 period under the prime ministership of a man called Gombos.
And that was a time when they seriously started to restrict educationwise, businesswise, connectionwise the people.
You know, it was basically the Nuremberg laws, which in somewhat may be different format were instituted in Hungary, Hungary being at all times an ally of Germany, of course.
So I remember in later years, in '37, '38 and so on, that they-- people overnight used a certain chemical on shop-- Jewish shop windows.
We didn't have a shop, so it didn't affect us personally.
And they put on Jew in Hungarian, zsido, and they put on it a writing-- it wasn't graffiti.
It was a chemical which went into the glass and you could not remove it.
Say, don't buy, this is a Jewish shop, that sort of things, more or less, which I think you may have seen in a different form, which the Nazis did in Berlin and other German cities.
So the intimidation slowly but surely progressed.
And it was really the attitude of the Jews, I presume, that this is another passing period and times will become better.
Do you remember what you felt at that time or--
I would say to you this, that the-- one was basically mentally educated to accept it, because we did not have pogroms.
We had a systematic program of-- to submit the Jews to all sorts of economic and other indignities, and make their life more difficult gradually.
It wasn't a matter which-- that you woke up one morning and suddenly you couldn't go on the street.
No.
You could carry on your life.
You were fooled into a security, which really was getting worse by months, by years.
And you got mentally into a frame of mind that you didn't consider it's terrible.
It was just another event.
And so it went.
Today when I look back on it, I realize how naive the whole mentality was.
I can't even say that anybody who would've-- be-- let it be American Jews or in England, would have been put to the same that they would have acted very differently, because what should you do?
You take a gun out?
Your throw a hand grenade?
It was no reason for it.
So there were times when Jews were oppressed in countries.
And there came a time when things became better.
You did not realize that this was a systematic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] way, which led eventually to the Final Solution.
And obviously-- however clever and however clear it was, obviously there were people-- I remember in our town, in 1937, the family called-- he was a timber merchant.
He lived in our street, emigrated to Palestine, [INAUDIBLE].
There were some families who rightly, correctly, anticipated the worst.
But they were in such minority that I think in our community of a few hundred families, maybe two or three families left.
It could have been partly influenced by the fact that it was difficult to get in.
British did not give an extending help, maybe under Arab pressure in the '30s, the mufti and all that.
And also, may have been due to the extended families people had, leave behind grandparents or children or brothers and so on.
It wasn't a simple just pick yourself up and go, and forget about your past, your family, and everything.
In those days, to go from Hungary to Palestine was a very long way.
You didn't have flights in two hours or three hours to get there.
And it was basically meant to be maybe for a lifetime.
So they could have been personal reasons, maybe there was somebody ill in the family or whatever, that families like to keep together, at least those days, they did.
And nobody was looking to break up a family.
Unfortunately, in our part of the world, people were extremely correct, too correct I would say, to the point of having their honor placed in the hands of the Hungarian management of the country.
And once there were many-- as you told me yourself, your parents or grandparents came from Russia-- we did not have this mass immigrations coming due to pogroms.
And the only people practically-- and I've got to be excused if I make a mistake, because there were very nice people who left as well for different reasons or had the sense to escape in time.
The people who did something incorrect, let's say they passed a check which wasn't met, they could not stay in that town anymore.
People-- the Jews or the non-Jews were not prepared to deal with them.
So what did they do?
They went up to Budapest.
It was a big city and they could get lost.
By the time somebody did something serious, then you really had to run away to America.
So practically, what I am trying to say is that after the war, I had absolutely no relations on anywhere near or far away.
Apparently, I'm told, that there is a film producer called Weiler-- his name I've seen many times on films-- that he seemingly is a relation of ours.
But to be honest with you, as he wasn't looking for me, I wasn't looking for him, maybe not nice of me, but as I don't want to have any favors, as I'm perfectly OK without it.
But I'm told that he was apparently some distant family of ours, whose maybe parents or grandparents emigrated to America.
And he spells his name with a Y, not W-E-I-L-E-R.
Now, the events of the pre-war period had various personal anecdotes.

And if I just may for a second refer to my notes, because once I remember things, I want to give you all the details in a more chronological order that it makes sense to a person who hears the story for the first time.
It certainly makes sense to me.
Now coming to the difficulties created by this rampant, ongoing anti-Semitism, I want to tell in our town the specific division and mentality of the population.
The Hungarians hated the Jews, because they considered them aliens.
The German minority hated them for the same reason.
They were quite well to do, the German minorities.
Mainly agriculture holdings, they had, quite well-to-do.
And they were called bohnenzuchter-- ponzichter, which means bohnenzuchter, which the exact translation is bean-growers, Because it was a bean-growing area.
It was a very good agriculturally rich area.
And they obviously were influenced by events taking on in Germany.
These people didn't even speak German.
I mean, I as a child spoke better German than Hungarian.
Obviously I went to Hungarian schools, so that wore off a little bit and probably my grammar is better in Hungarian than in German.
But today I don't know, maybe probably in English.
So that area, these people, was not too advisable for Jews to communicate.
Far as I know, that my father spoke the dialect, which was a-- a different German dialect than the normal [NON-ENGLISH] German, normal German, which is spoken, and traded with them.
But at the same time, they were vehemently anti-Semitic.
The-- I remember that in 1941, at a later point, there was a whole SS brigade or battalion put together from volunteers of the sons of these people.
The-- I watched a year later the Red Cross trains bringing them back injured, and some mutilated, from the Russian front.
Nevertheless, the-- the-- the hatred against Jews was constantly if not on top of the surface, certainly under it.
Now, the interesting period of my schooling time is also that I finished my primary school in the summer of 1940.
And I was supposed to start school in September.
Those days in this gymnasium, there was also another lyceum, which was a Protestant grammar school.
And then there was the [NON-ENGLISH], they called them.
That was the Catholic priests school, also a secular school, but it was run by priests.

And then there was the-- what you could call in England the comprehensive school, where all of the people who were not academically really up to it went for four years and that was the end of their education.
Now, the Jewish children were excluded from the two non-denominational-- because I would call the lyceum was also more-- less-- it wasn't so religiously influenced like the Catholic monastery school.
There were allowed in a class of 30, one Jewish child.

Now my brother, my cousins-- one of my cousins who later became an eminent professor in philosophy studied in Oxford and was a professor at Tel Aviv University-- all came-- all went to that school, even my parent-- my father and his brothers as well, so my family was desperate to get me into that school.
But again, it's anti-Jewish regulation.
It was practically impossible.
There was no appeal against it.
The schools got their instructions from the Minister of Education and they acted upon.

The story goes on there that I have been kept at home by my parents, which was against the law, for a month, in the month of September 1940, until representations were made by my uncle, the lawyer, and my father went to Budapest to see the parliamentary representative of Sopron, who happened to be Count Csaky, who was the foreign minister.

And because of the family's connection, the intervention I think was the sole and only case which I have ever heard of where the foreign ministry gave instructions to the Minister of Education to instruct the Szechenyi Istvan gymnasium to admit me, which they did in October, 1940.
